mnemonics.title: Heavy Metal Hazards
mnemonics.content: L.M.C.H - Lead causes Liver damage, Mercury affects Mind, Cadmium attacks Cadmium-damaged Kidneys (Kidney), Chromium causes Cancer

acronyms.usage: Policy making producers responsible for end-of-life collection and recycling of their products
EU directive limiting use of 10 hazardous substances in electronics
EU directive for collection and recycling of electronic waste
Old monitor technology containing leaded glass
Toxic industrial chemicals found in old electrical equipment
Flame-retarding chemicals in electronics that persist in environment
Discarded electrical and electronic devices at end of useful life
Refrigerant gases that deplete ozone layer found in old cooling appliances

visualTips.visualization: Picture a device hierarchy: CRT monitors have most lead, circuit boards contain gold/silver, batteries hold cadmium/lithium, capacitors contain PCBs, cables have PVC plastic
Think of flowchart: E-waste → Informal recycling (burning/acid) → Toxic release → Air/soil/water contamination → Food chain → Human health impacts
Pyramid with 5 levels: Prevention (top, best) → Reuse → Recycling → Energy Recovery → Disposal (bottom, worst)

analogies.analogy: Like unexploded bombs, e-waste contains toxic substances that 'detonate' health problems when improperly handled

analogies.concept: Informal recycling as poisoning the well
analogies.analogy: Just as poisoning a shared well harms entire community, improper e-waste recycling contaminates shared environment affecting everyone

analogies.concept: Urban mining as reverse treasure hunting
analogies.analogy: Instead of mining earth for metals, we 'mine' discarded devices - like finding treasure in your own backyard
